response: "{\"session_id\":\"1eqO9Kk11kxcsjYH\",\"results\":[{\"source_id\":\"dnpuocNEzBUYxgOKWBuda\",\"url\":\"https://partner.microsoft.com/en-us/solutions/nonprofit\",\"title\":\"Nonprofit Partner Readiness\",\"publish_date\":null,\"excerpts\":[\"# Nonprofit partner readiness\\n## Industry solutions\\nBrowse nonprofit tools, solutions, and resources.\\n[Browse](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/industry/nonprofit/ \\\"Explore\\\")\\n\\n...\\n\\n## Solution play readiness materials\\n### Innovate with Low code AI & Agents\\n#### Pricing & Licensing\\nDiscover nonprofit pricing and licensing to scale, modernize, and maximize impact.\\n* [Explore resources](https://partner.microsoft.com/en-us/solutions/nonprofit/pricing-and-licensing)\\n\\n...\\n\\n## Community updates\\n### [Microsoft Nonprofit Partner Community Update | November 2025](https://assetsprod.microsoft.com/mpn/en-us/microsoft-nonprofit-partner-community-update-november-2025.pdf)\\n#### [FY26 H1 Nonprofit Partner Kickoff Asia Present...[truncated 22452 chars]... of the nearly half-million nonprofits who have benefited from the program have about two months to either find new room in their budgets to pay for the products—this at a time of unprecedented crisis for the sector including threatened and actual [cuts to federal funding](https://nonprofitquarterly.org/nonprofits-fight-trump-funding-freeze-in-federal-court/) ; or to find alternatives.\\n“We are retiring the Microsoft 365 Business Premium and Office 365 E1 grant offers.”\\nThe sudden deadline creates a potential secondary crisis for nonprofits unable to find ways to pay for the products in time: The possibility of losing untold amounts of company data and other work products dependent on the Microsoft software.\\n\\n...\\n\\nSpeaking with _NPQ_ , Weiner said that for thousands of nonprofits, especially the many already existing on shoestring budgets, the Microsoft grant program was a lifeline that much of the sector had come to depend upon to be able to perform basic work tasks.\"]}]}"

COLORADO

[Nate Trela](/staff/7931822001/nate-trela/)

Fort Collins Coloradoan

The threshold to be considered middle class on average in Colorado is increasing faster than the rest of the country.

SmartAsset, an online platform for financial advice and information, once again calculated the [middle-class income ranges](https://smartasset.com/data-studies/middle-class-2026) for 100 major U.S. cities — including three in Colorado — and all 50 states.

Colorado as a whole finished with the eighth-highest upper limit on what makes someone middle class, [up a spot from 2024](/story/news/local/colorado/2025/03/10/what-middle-class-is-in-colorado-in-2025/81947539007/).

But the threshold in Colorado’s largest cities didn’t climb as fast as in peer cities — and for a change, Denver was not the one with the highest threshold in the state.

The calculations are based on Pew Research Center’s definition of middle income...[truncated 999 chars]...le class in Colorado Springs?

Colorado Springs is ranked 42nd, with households needing to make between $55,781 and $167,344. The median household income is $83,672.

## What states have the highest middle class incomes?

The three states with the highest thresholds to be considered middle class are unchanged from 2025, but the upper limits to be middle class in those states went up:

1. Massachusetts: $69,885 to $209,656
2. New Jersey: $69,529 to $208,588
3. Maryland: $68,603 to $205,810
4. Hawaii: $67,163 to $201,490
5. California: $66,766 to $200,298

## What cities have the highest middle class incomes?

Four cities passed 2025’s leader, Arlington, Virginia, on the list.

1. San Jose, California: $98,817 to $296,452
2. Irvine, California: $97,154 to $291,462
3. Frisco, Texas: $96,963 to $290,888
4. San Francisco: $93,201 to $279,602
5. Arlington, Virginia: $89,055 to $267,164

*Nate Trela covers trending news in Colorado and Utah for the USA TODAY Network.*"
